cases.title: Certificate rotation for intermittently connected gateways

public.objective: Rotate a fleet certificate before expiry without permanently stranding intermittently connected gateways or preserving trust in a compromised key.
facts: The current fleet certificate expires in twenty-one days.
Eighteen percent of gateways have historically remained offline for more than thirty days.
Gateways can store two trust chains, but dual validation adds six percent to handshake CPU.
The old private key is suspected of exposure, but no confirmed misuse has been observed.
A recovery image can install a new trust chain, but physical service costs about 180 dollars per site.
public.decision_request: Choose the trust-overlap policy, offline-device recovery path, emergency revocation trigger, and completion evidence.
plausible_paths: Revoke the old chain immediately and require physical recovery for offline gateways.
Run a bounded dual-trust window with an emergency kill path.
Keep the old chain trusted until every gateway has checked in.
material_constraints: Some gateways will remain offline beyond certificate expiry.
An exposed old key may require revocation before fleet migration completes.
Dual validation consumes scarce gateway CPU.
Physical recovery is available but costly and slow.
Rollback cannot depend on the expired or revoked old chain.
harmful_failure_paths: A long-offline gateway reconnects with no trusted path and cannot receive recovery instructions.
The suspected old key remains accepted indefinitely while waiting for every device.
incomplete_information: The number of gateways that can receive the recovery image through an out-of-band channel is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Canary dual trust on one gateway cohort while simulating expiry, revocation, and long-offline reconnects.

must_cover_constraints: Separate normal overlap from the emergency response to confirmed key misuse.
Provide a recovery trust path for gateways offline beyond the migration window.
Bound the CPU and duration cost of dual validation.
Avoid making rollback depend on the old certificate remaining valid.
Distinguish fleet completion evidence from simple check-in counts.
acceptable_decision_families: A time-bounded dual-trust canary with an independent recovery chain and emergency revocation trigger.
Cohort migration with out-of-band recovery for long-offline gateways and a named decision point for old-key revocation.
fatal_errors: Trusting the suspected old key indefinitely until every gateway reconnects.
Revoking the old chain without any viable bootstrap path for long-offline gateways.
known_valid_insights: An independent recovery trust chain can preserve recoverability without extending normal trust in the old key.
decision_closure_requirements: Name the first gateway cohort.
Set overlap duration and CPU stop thresholds.
Define the confirmed-misuse revocation trigger.
Specify the long-offline recovery mechanism and owner.
Define completion evidence and rollback.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward a bounded trust transition that treats compromise response and offline recovery as separate controls.

cases.title: Queued data exports during account deletion

public.objective: Honor account deletion while preserving legitimate export delivery and auditable processing.
facts: Customers can request a data export and account deletion within the same session.
Large exports may remain queued for thirty-six hours.
Deletion policy requires production data removal within twenty-four hours.
Export workers snapshot record identifiers when a job starts and fetch the underlying data later.
Completed exports use signed download links valid for seventy-two hours.
public.decision_request: Choose job cancellation semantics, download-link behavior, deletion evidence, and rollout controls.
plausible_paths: Allow every export requested before deletion to finish.
Cancel queued exports and revoke completed links when deletion begins.
Pause deletion until every previously requested export expires.
material_constraints: Queued jobs can outlive the deletion deadline.
A worker snapshot does not contain all exported data.
Previously issued download links may expose deleted-account data.
Deletion must be auditable across the API, queue, workers, object storage, and link service.
A customer may reasonably expect an export requested immediately before deletion.
harmful_failure_paths: A delayed worker recreates export data after deletion has been recorded complete.
A still-valid signed link serves a completed export after the account is deleted.
incomplete_information: Legal has not decided whether a pre-deletion export must remain downloadable after deletion begins.
public.reversible_action: Pilot cancellation and link revocation for internal test accounts with delayed workers and forced retries.

must_cover_constraints: Coordinate deletion with queued, running, and retrying export workers.
Address both temporary export objects and already issued download links.
Avoid claiming legal entitlement while policy is unresolved.
Produce cross-service evidence that no worker recreated deleted data.
Define a customer-visible outcome for the conflicting export and deletion requests.
acceptable_decision_families: Deletion takes precedence with atomic job invalidation, link revocation, cleanup evidence, and clear customer notice.
A short explicitly approved export-completion exception with isolated storage, expiry, and deletion evidence, pending legal classification.
fatal_errors: Marking deletion complete while delayed workers can still materialize export objects.
Leaving signed export links usable after the governing deletion policy says access must end.
known_valid_insights: Queue cancellation alone is insufficient because running workers, retries, objects, and links have independent state.
decision_closure_requirements: Set precedence while legal classification is pending.
Name the cancellation and revocation owner.
Define worker fencing or deletion-version checks.
Specify object and link cleanup evidence.
Set pilot stop and rollback conditions.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward cross-service closure. Do not require the model to invent the unresolved legal classification.

cases.title: Payment provider failover with delayed webhooks

public.objective: Restore checkout during a provider outage without creating duplicate charges or unreconciled customer balances.
facts: The primary provider has experienced three outages longer than forty minutes this quarter.
A secondary provider supports cards but not bank transfers.
The secondary provider accepts merchant idempotency keys, but the current integration generates provider-specific keys.
Primary-provider authorization and settlement webhooks may arrive up to forty-eight hours late.
Finance can reconcile provider exports daily but not continuously.
public.decision_request: Choose the failover cohort, idempotency boundary, delayed-event handling, and failback criteria.
plausible_paths: Route every retry to the secondary provider after five minutes.
Fail over a bounded card cohort using a cross-provider payment-attempt key.
Keep checkout unavailable until the primary provider recovers.
material_constraints: Bank transfers cannot use the secondary provider.
The same customer attempt may reach both providers.
Late primary webhooks can arrive after secondary success.
Continuous reconciliation is unavailable.
Failback can overlap with in-flight secondary attempts.
harmful_failure_paths: A retry succeeds at the secondary provider after the primary provider already authorized it.
Traffic fails back while secondary attempts remain unresolved, splitting one order across providers.
incomplete_information: The fraction of apparently failed primary authorizations that later settle is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Run a shadow failover simulation against recorded outage traffic without submitting charges.

must_cover_constraints: Use an idempotency identity that spans both providers and customer retries.
Handle late primary authorizations after a secondary outcome.
Keep unsupported payment methods out of the secondary route.
Bound exposure while continuous reconciliation is unavailable.
Prevent failback from racing unresolved secondary attempts.
acceptable_decision_families: A card-only canary with a durable cross-provider attempt ledger, delayed-webhook reconciliation, and guarded failback.
Shadow validation followed by tightly capped failover only after cross-provider idempotency and repair procedures pass.
fatal_errors: Retrying payment attempts across providers without a shared idempotency boundary.
Failing traffic back before unresolved attempts and late primary events are reconciled.
known_valid_insights: Provider-local idempotency does not protect a payment attempt that crosses provider boundaries.
decision_closure_requirements: Name the first card cohort and volume cap.
Define the cross-provider attempt key and owner.
Specify delayed-webhook reconciliation and customer remediation.
Set failover stop conditions.
Define failback evidence and rollback.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward a bounded failover that prioritizes duplicate-charge prevention over nominal checkout availability.

cases.title: Automated revocation of stale project access

public.objective: Reduce stale project access without disrupting emergency response or removing still-required contractor access.
facts: The company has about thirty thousand project-level grants.
Manager reviews are sixty days overdue for twenty-two percent of grants.
Contractors frequently move between projects before HR records update.
SCIM removal can take up to six hours to reach two legacy applications.
Emergency responders use a separate time-limited elevation system, but some teams also retain standing project access.
public.decision_request: Choose the automation scope, exception path, propagation evidence, and expansion criteria.
plausible_paths: Start with low-risk inactive grants and require bounded exceptions.
Continue manual reviews until HR and SCIM data are real time.
material_constraints: Review overdue status does not prove that access is unused.
Contractor assignment data may lag actual work.
Legacy application revocation is asynchronous.
Emergency elevation exists but is not the only current incident-access path.
Rollback must not silently recreate grants in applications that already processed revocation.
harmful_failure_paths: Automation removes an active responder's only working access during an incident.
The control plane records revocation while a legacy application still permits access.
incomplete_information: The percentage of standing incident access that can safely migrate to emergency elevation is unknown.
public.reversible_action: Canary warnings and simulated revocations for inactive low-risk grants before enforcing removals.

must_cover_constraints: Define low-risk inactivity using more than overdue review status.
Protect active work when contractor assignment records lag.
Verify application-side revocation rather than trusting the control-plane request.
Separate emergency elevation migration from ordinary stale-access cleanup.
Make rollback reconcile actual downstream grant state.
acceptable_decision_families: Warning-only shadow mode followed by low-risk revocation with owner-approved exceptions and downstream proof.
A narrow inactive-grant canary while incident access is inventoried and migrated separately.
fatal_errors: Automatically revoking every overdue grant without validating active use or assignment.
Treating a submitted SCIM removal as proof that downstream access ended.
known_valid_insights: Emergency elevation can reduce standing access, but it must be validated before standing incident grants are removed.
decision_closure_requirements: Define the first low-risk cohort.
Name exception approval and expiry.
Set downstream propagation evidence and timeout.
Specify incident-access protection.
Define stop conditions, rollback reconciliation, and expansion evidence.
adjudication.reasoning_notes: Reward phased enforcement with downstream truth. Do not equate overdue governance metadata with proven stale access.
